title: Surgical Management of Urinary Stones — SWL and Ureteroscopy
subtitle: Block C — Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy, semirigid and flexible ureteroscopy, laser lithotripsy
guidelines_version: EAU Guidelines on Urolithiasis 2024 · AUA/Endourology Society Surgical Management of Stones 2016 (amended 2022)
textbook_refs: Türk C et al. EAU Guidelines on Urolithiasis (2024)
Assimos D et al. Surgical Management of Stones: AUA/Endourology Society Guideline (2016, amended 2022)

learning_objectives: Describe the mechanism, patient selection, contraindications, and expected outcomes of SWL
Select appropriate stone management strategy based on size, position, composition, and patient factors
Explain semirigid and flexible ureteroscopy technique, stone fragmentation options, and complications
Apply EAU 2024 evidence-based recommendations for renal stone treatment by size category
Manage post-ureteroscopy complications including ureteral avulsion, ureteral perforation, and steinstrasse

sections.title: Extracorporeal Shock Wave Lithotripsy (SWL): Mechanism, Equipment, and Contraindications

blocks.content:
**Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (SWL)** was introduced by Chaussy et al. in 1980 and revolutionised stone management, offering the first non-invasive stone fragmentation technique. SWL generates acoustic shock waves extracorporeally that are focused on the stone using imaging guidance (fluoroscopy or ultrasound). Shock waves are generated by three main mechanisms: electrohydraulic (spark gap — original Dornier HM3; broad focal zone, higher energy, best results historically), electromagnetic (coil — most common modern devices; narrow focal zone, more reproducible), and piezoelectric (wide aperture, narrow focal zone, lower peak pressure). The **HM3 Dornier lithotripter** remains the gold standard against which other devices are compared, despite being technically obsolete.

Shock wave stone fragmentation occurs through two principal mechanisms: (1) **Direct compressive stress** — the leading shock wave creates compressive stress at the proximal stone surface; (2) **Cavitation** — the negative pressure tail of the shock wave causes microbubble formation that collapses violently, creating tensile spalling forces on the distal stone surface. Repeated cycles crack the stone progressively from the surface toward the centre. Effective fragmentation requires precise focusing — even slight misalignment significantly reduces efficacy.

**Stone localisation** during SWL: fluoroscopy is used for radiopaque stones; real-time ultrasound for radiolucent (uric acid) or poorly opaque (cystine) stones. Target accuracy requirements: focal zone diameter of modern lithotripters is 5–15 mm — patient movement and respiratory excursion must be controlled.

**Absolute contraindications to SWL**: (1) Pregnancy — shock waves cause fetal tissue injury; (2) Uncontrolled coagulopathy or anticoagulant therapy that cannot be held; (3) Ipsilateral obstructing lesion distal to the stone (fragments cannot clear); (4) Aortic or renal artery aneurysm; (5) Severe uncorrected urinary infection/sepsis (high-pressure fragmentation can disseminate bacteria); (6) Non-functional ipsilateral kidney (no point fragmenting if kidney cannot drain). Relative contraindications: pacemaker (risk of arrhythmia with electrohydraulic systems — place SWL at safe distance, cardiologist input; not absolute with modern devices and synchronisation), obesity (limits penetration depth), anatomical abnormalities (horseshoe kidney — increased SWL complications, though SWL is sometimes still used), skeletal abnormalities limiting positioning, coagulopathy correctable pre-SWL.

blocks.title: SWL Contraindications and Alternative Options
rows: Fetal tissue injury from shock waves
Ureteroscopy (2nd trimester preferred) or ureteral stent + deferred treatment
Ureteroscopy or PCNL; treat aneurysm first
Treat infection first; then SWL or URS
No clinical benefit; fragments cannot clear
Electromagnetic/piezoelectric lithotripter with cardiologist standby; or URS
Focal depth limitation (>12–13 cm); reduced efficacy

blocks.title: Board Pearl — SWL Absolute Contraindications
blocks.content: Memorise the absolute SWL contraindications: PAUCOD — Pregnancy, Anticoagulation uncorrected, Urosepsis/infection, Contralateral obstruction, Obstruction distal to stone, Disorder of coagulation (aneurysm). Pregnancy and uncontrolled anticoagulation are the most commonly tested. SWL in pregnancy causes fetal injury regardless of fetal shielding — always contraindicated.

sections.title: SWL Outcomes: Stone-Free Rates, Predictors, and Complications

blocks.content:
**SWL stone-free rates** (EAU 2024) vary substantially by stone size, position, and composition: Renal pelvic stones <10 mm: 90–95%; Renal pelvic stones 10–20 mm: 65–80%; Lower-pole renal stones <10 mm: 60–75% (lower than upper/middle calyces due to inadequate fragment drainage from the lower pole — anatomy-dependent); Lower-pole stones >10 mm: 45–60%. Ureteral stones <10 mm: proximal 70–85%, distal 85–95%.

**Predictors of poor SWL outcome**: (1) Stone size >20 mm — SWL fails as monotherapy; PCNL preferred; (2) Lower-pole location — poor drainage of fragments; (3) High stone density on CT — Hounsfield units >1000 HU indicate calcium oxalate monohydrate or brushite stones that are highly resistant to shock wave fragmentation; (4) Long skin-to-stone distance >100 mm (obesity) — reduced energy delivered to stone; (5) Stone composition — cystine and brushite are SWL-resistant; uric acid and calcium oxalate dihydrate are most fragile; (6) Calyceal anatomy — long, narrow calyceal infundibulum impairs fragment clearance; (7) Infundibulopelvic angle <70° in lower pole — impairs gravity-dependent fragment drainage.

**SWL complications**: (1) **Steinstrasse** (German: 'stone street') — column of stone fragments obstructing the ureter after SWL for renal stones; occurs in 2–4% overall, higher risk with larger stones. Management: most resolve spontaneously; obstructive/septic steinstrasse requires ureteroscopy or ureteral stenting; (2) **Perinephric haematoma** — subcapsular/perinephric bleeding from shock wave-induced renal parenchymal injury; symptomatic in 1–2%, significant (requiring intervention) in <0.1%; associated with anticoagulation, multiple sessions, large number of shocks; (3) **Renal hypertension** — rare, debated — from renal scarring post-SWL; (4) **Bacteriuria/urosepsis** — particularly if treating infected stones; pre-SWL urine culture and antibiotic cover essential; (5) **Cardiac arrhythmia** — during SWL, R-wave synchronisation of shock delivery reduces arrhythmia risk; (6) **Pain** — during SWL, managed with IV sedation/analgesia; epidural anaesthesia (original HM3 required it — modern devices do not).

blocks.title: Critical Warning — SWL for Lower-Pole Stones
blocks.content: Lower-pole renal stones have significantly worse SWL stone-free rates compared to other calyceal positions due to impaired fragment drainage against gravity. For lower-pole stones <10 mm, SWL achieves only 60–75% stone-free rate versus 90–95% for renal pelvic stones of equivalent size. For lower-pole stones >10 mm, EAU 2024 recommends ureteroscopy (flexible) or PCNL over SWL. Patient position (prone, lateral) or mechanical percussion/inversion therapy after SWL may improve lower-pole fragment clearance but evidence is limited.

sections.title: Ureteroscopy: Technique, Instruments, and Laser Lithotripsy

blocks.content:
**Ureteroscopy (URS)** provides direct endoscopic access to the ureteral and renal collecting system for stone visualisation, fragmentation, and extraction. Two main instrument types: **semirigid ureteroscopes** (6–8 Fr diameter; rigid shaft; limited deflection; best suited for distal and proximal ureteral stones) and **flexible ureteroscopes** (digital or fibreoptic; 7.5–9 Fr working channel; full active deflection 270° for access to all renal calyces including lower pole).

**Technique**: Pre-operative ureteral stenting (2–4 weeks) facilitates ureteral passive dilation and reduces access complications, though stentless access is increasingly used by experienced endourologists. A safety guidewire is placed before ureteroscope insertion — this is maintained throughout the procedure and provides a safety route for stent/catheter placement if complications occur. A **ureteral access sheath (UAS)** (11–13 Fr) can be placed over the guidewire to facilitate repeated ureteroscope passages and improve irrigation flow, reducing intrarenal pressure during retrograde irrigation.

**Intrarenal pelvic pressure** during flexible URS is a critical safety concept. Elevated intrarenal pressure (>30–40 cmH₂O) during irrigation in an infected collecting system causes bacteraemia — analogous to the infected obstructed kidney scenario. UAS use significantly reduces intrarenal pressure by providing an outflow channel. Pyelovenous/pyelolymphatic backflow with infected urine is the mechanism of post-URS sepsis.

**Stone fragmentation**: **Holmium:YAG (Ho:YAG) laser** (2140 nm wavelength — absorbed by water) is the gold standard for laser lithotripsy. Mechanism: photothermal — rapid water absorption causes explosive microcrater formation (not primarily cavitation). Advantages: fragments all stone compositions regardless of hardness; precise tissue interaction with minimal thermal spread (short pulse duration); usable through flexible ureteroscope (200–365 μm fibres). Settings: high-energy, low-frequency ('Moses mode' — newer pulse technology reducing retropulsion) for efficient fragmentation; low-energy, high-frequency ('dusting' — vaporises stone into sub-200 μm particles that pass spontaneously, reducing need for basketing). **Thulium fibre laser (TFL)** (1940 nm) — emerging technology; superior tissue-water absorption efficiency; lower retropulsion; superior stone-free rates in early RCTs. **Pneumatic lithotripsy** (ballistic: rigid probe, generates pneumatic impulse) — effective for ureteral stones but cannot be used through flexible ureteroscopes and causes significant stone retropulsion. **Electrohydraulic lithotripsy (EHL)** — now rarely used (risk of urothelial injury).

**Stone extraction**: after laser fragmentation, fragments are retrieved with nitinol baskets (NTrap, NGage). Routine basket retrieval of all fragments versus laser dusting (leaving small fragments to pass spontaneously) represents a current area of debate — EAU 2024 notes equivalent stone-free rates for stones <2 cm with both approaches in experienced hands.

blocks.title: Lithotripsy Modalities for Ureteroscopy: Comparison
rows: Very effective, no special fibre needed
Softer stones — CaOx dihydrate, uric acid

blocks.title: Board Pearl — Ho:YAG Laser Mechanism
blocks.content: Holmium:YAG laser wavelength 2140 nm — absorbed predominantly by water (not stone colour-dependent, unlike older laser types). Mechanism is PHOTOTHERMAL (steam bubble formation in the water layer at the stone surface) — not photomechanical. This is why Ho:YAG fragments all stone types regardless of composition, including the hardest cystine and calcium oxalate monohydrate stones. Contrast with flash-lamp pulsed dye laser (504 nm) — only fragments calcium oxalate stones (colour-dependent, historical).

sections.title: EAU 2024 Treatment Selection: Renal Stones by Size

blocks.content:
EAU 2024 provides evidence-based treatment recommendations for renal stones stratified by size:

**Renal stones <10 mm**: observation (with regular imaging surveillance ×6–12 months) is appropriate for asymptomatic, non-growing, non-obstructing stones in patients without high-risk features. Active treatment: SWL (first-line for most locations) or flexible ureteroscopy (f-URS). Stone-free rates: SWL 86–90% (upper/middle calyces), 60–75% (lower pole); f-URS 90–95% (all locations). EAU recommends f-URS over SWL for lower-pole stones ≤10 mm (superior stone-free rates). For non-lower pole stones <10 mm: both SWL and f-URS are equivalent — patient preference, stone composition, and institutional expertise guide choice.

**Renal stones 10–20 mm**: SWL or f-URS are both recommended for upper and middle calyceal stones. Stone-free rates: SWL 65–80%; f-URS 85–95%. For lower-pole stones 10–20 mm: EAU recommends f-URS or PCNL over SWL (poor SWL outcomes in lower pole with this size range). PCNL is appropriate if stone burden is high or multiple stones.

**Renal stones >20 mm**: PCNL is the primary recommended treatment. Stone-free rates: PCNL 80–90%; f-URS 60–75% (requires multiple sessions for large stones); SWL <50% (inadequate for large stone burden). EAU 2024: PCNL Grade A recommendation for stones >20 mm. Retrograde intrarenal surgery (RIRS/f-URS) is an alternative for >20 mm stones in selected patients (those unfit for PCNL, anticoagulated, preference) with lower single-session stone-free rates but comparable cumulative stone-free rates after multiple sessions.

**Staghorn calculi**: PCNL monotherapy or in combination is the gold standard. SWL monotherapy for staghorn calculi is not recommended (stone-free rates <50%; high steinstrasse risk; multiple sessions required with increasing complications). Open pyelolithotomy is now reserved for exceptional cases (anatomical abnormalities, failed PCNL, horseshoe kidney with unusual anatomy).

blocks.title: EAU 2024 Renal Stone Treatment Recommendations by Size and Location
rows: f-URS or PCNL if dissolution fails

blocks.title: Clinical Context — Choosing Between SWL and f-URS for a 15 mm Upper Calyx Stone
blocks.content: Both SWL and f-URS are acceptable first-line for a 15 mm upper calyx stone. Factors favouring SWL: patient prefers non-invasive approach, stone HU <900 (softer, more SWL-responsive), no concurrent infection, no anticoagulation. Factors favouring f-URS: stone HU >1000 (hard — poor SWL candidate), cystine stone composition, patient desires single-procedure clearance, anatomical abnormality limiting SWL access, previous SWL failure at this site, patient on anticoagulation unable to hold. Discuss both options with the patient and document the shared decision.

sections.title: Ureteral Stone Management: SWL vs Ureteroscopy

blocks.content:
Ureteral stones are managed by either observation (≤10 mm, no complications, see Chapter C1), SWL, or ureteroscopy. The choice is guided by stone size, position, duration of obstruction, and patient factors.

**Proximal ureteral stones (above iliac vessels)**: SWL (stone-free rate 70–85% for stones <10 mm) and ureteroscopy (stone-free rate 80–90%) are both options. SWL is less invasive but requires adequate fragmentation and stone passage; semirigid ureteroscopy provides direct stone access with higher single-session stone-free rates. Flexible ureteroscopy is used for proximal stones that cannot be reached with semirigid instruments. EAU 2024: for proximal ureteral stones <10 mm: SWL or semirigid URS equally recommended; >10 mm: URS preferred.

**Distal ureteral stones (below iliac vessels)**: SWL (stone-free rate 85–95% for <10 mm) and ureteroscopy (stone-free rate 95–97%) are both effective. Ureteroscopy provides higher stone-free rates in a single session. EAU 2024 supports either SWL or URS for distal ureteral stones <10 mm. For >10 mm distal stones: URS preferred.

**Special situations**: Impacted ureteral stones (long-standing obstruction, significant hydronephrosis, urothelial oedema around stone) — ureteroscopy preferred over SWL; oedematous mucosa around the stone reduces SWL efficacy and increases risk of ureteral mucosal injury during spontaneous fragment passage. Steinstrasse after SWL — small distal fragment causing the obstruction should be treated with ureteroscopy; the proximal column of fragments may clear spontaneously or after ureteral stenting. Large steinstrasse: URS with laser fragmentation of the lead fragment.

**Ureteral stenting post-URS**: routine ureteral stent after uncomplicated ureteroscopy without ureteral injury is not mandatory (EAU 2024 Grade B — stentless URS is acceptable for uncomplicated procedures). Stent is recommended after: ureteral injury, difficult access, incomplete stone clearance with risk of steinstrasse, single kidney, concurrent UTI treated, planned second-look procedure. If stent is placed: alpha-blocker (tamsulosin) reduces stent-related LUTS symptoms and should be offered to all stented patients.

blocks.title: Critical Warning — Ureteral Avulsion During URS
blocks.content: Ureteral avulsion is the most serious complication of ureteroscopy — complete or near-complete separation of the ureter, typically from overly forceful basket extraction of a stone that is too large to pass the ureter. Clinical features: loss of vision, haematuria, loss of resistance, recognition of white tissue. Management: immediate recognition + ureteral stent placement over the safety guidewire to secure the injury; definitive surgical repair (uretero-ureterostomy, ureteroneocystostomy, Boari flap, or ileal interposition depending on level and extent). Prevention: never extract a stone that requires excessive traction — fragment first with laser.

Ureteroscopy complications are classified as intraoperative (immediate) and post-operative. The Clavien-Dindo classification is used for systematic complication reporting in urology.

**Intraoperative complications**: (1) **Ureteral perforation** — small defect from guidewire or ureteroscope tip; managed with ureteral stent placement and cessation of procedure; major perforation requires immediate stenting and possible open/laparoscopic repair; (2) **Ureteral avulsion** — most serious; management as above; (3) **Failure to access** — inability to advance the ureteroscope past a tight ureter (common in first-time URS without prior stenting); management: balloon dilation or passive ureteral dilation with pre-placed stent 2–4 weeks; (4) **Stone migration** — stone pushed retrograde into the renal pelvis ('stone migration' or 'push-up'); common with pneumatic lithotripsy (high retropulsion); management: flexible ureteroscopy in same session or deferred; Moses/TFL laser reduces retropulsion; (5) **Mucosal tear** — superficial laceration of ureteral mucosa; usually managed conservatively with stenting.

**Post-operative complications**: (1) **Fever/urosepsis** — occurs in 0.3–5.5% despite prophylactic antibiotics; risk factors include pre-existing bacteriuria, infected stone (struvite), elevated intrarenal pressure during irrigation, long procedure time. Prevention: pre-operative urine culture and treatment, antibiotic prophylaxis, UAS use to reduce intrarenal pressure; (2) **Haematuria** — common (almost universal), usually self-limiting over 24–48 hours; significant haematuria requiring intervention is rare (<0.5%); (3) **Ureteral stricture** (late, 0.5–1.5%) — delayed complication from ischaemic injury during overly aggressive dilation, mucosal stripping, or thermal injury from laser; presents at 3–12 months with obstructive LUTS and reduced Qmax; diagnosed with ureterogram or URS; management: endoscopic balloon dilation or urethroplasty for significant strictures; (4) **Ureteral stent symptoms** — if stent placed, 60–80% of patients experience significant stent-related LUTS (frequency, urgency, dysuria, haematuria, flank pain on voiding); alpha-blockers (tamsulosin) and antimuscarinics significantly reduce stent symptoms (EAU Grade B recommendation).

**Mucosal safety** during laser lithotripsy: Ho:YAG laser in contact with urothelium can cause thermal mucosal injury. Safe use: keep laser fibre visible at all times, fire only when fibre is in contact with stone (not urothelium), use the minimum effective power setting, maintain adequate irrigation flow to dissipate heat.

blocks.title: URS Complications: Classification, Incidence, and Management
rows: Emergency stenting over guidewire; surgical repair
Passive dilation stent 2–4 weeks; reschedule URS
Flexible URS same session or deferred; laser preferred
IV antibiotics; blood cultures; aggressive resuscitation
Endoscopic balloon dilation; urethroplasty for significant stricture

sections.title: Post-URS Follow-Up, Stone-Free Assessment, and Stent Management

blocks.content:
**Stone-free status assessment** after ureteroscopy: defined as no residual stone fragments on imaging. Imaging modality: NCCT is the most sensitive (detects fragments >1 mm); ultrasound and plain KUB are less sensitive for small residual fragments. EAU 2024 recommends imaging at 4–6 weeks post-URS for confirmation of stone-free status; earlier imaging if symptomatic. **Clinically insignificant residual fragments (CIRFs)** — defined as fragments <4 mm on imaging after stone surgery — were previously considered safe to monitor, but EAU 2024 notes that 20–40% of CIRFs grow or cause symptoms over 5-year follow-up, warranting active surveillance imaging every 6–12 months.

**Ureteral stent removal**: if a stent is placed post-URS, removal is typically at 2–4 weeks by flexible cystoscopy or, if a pull string is attached, by the patient themselves at home (patient-controlled stent removal reduces healthcare visits and costs — evidence from the USSOP trial). Stents should not be left in situ beyond the planned duration without clinical review — encrustation and biofilm formation increase exponentially after 4–6 weeks, making removal increasingly difficult.

**Metabolic follow-up** (Chapter C1 protocol): all stone formers require 24-hour urine collection at 8–12 weeks post-treatment (after recovery) and annually thereafter. This timing allows assessment of baseline metabolic status without the confounding effects of acute stone episode (illness changes fluid intake, diet, and metabolism). Lifestyle modifications and pharmacotherapy are adjusted based on metabolic follow-up results.

**Repeat stone events**: patients must receive clear written and verbal instructions regarding signs of recurrence (renal colic, haematuria, fever) and when to attend the emergency department. Urology follow-up at 6 months post-stone episode for stone formers, then annually for recurrent formers or those on preventive therapy.

blocks.title: Board Pearl — Stone-Free Definition and CIRF
blocks.content: Stone-free: no residual fragments OR residual fragments <4 mm (clinically insignificant residual fragments, CIRFs). However, EAU 2024 emphasises that CIRFs are NOT truly insignificant — 20–40% grow or cause symptoms over 5 years. NCCT at 4–6 weeks post-treatment is the gold standard for stone-free confirmation. Never accept 'stone-free' without post-operative imaging — even in apparently successful procedures.

consolidation.intro: Test your knowledge of SWL and ureteroscopy stone management with 20 board-style questions.

questions.stem: A 44-year-old woman at 12 weeks gestation presents with a 7 mm left ureteral stone at the ureteropelvic junction causing moderate hydronephrosis and intermittent pain. She is afebrile with normal WBC and creatinine. What is the most appropriate management?

options.A: Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy
options.B: Analgesia (paracetamol), observation, and ureteral stent if pain is uncontrolled
options.C: Immediate ureteroscopy with Ho:YAG laser lithotripsy
options.D: Non-contrast CT KUB to confirm stone position before deciding

explanation.correct: In pregnancy, the first principle is to minimise fetal risk. Initial management: analgesia with paracetamol (safe all trimesters), hydration, and observation — most ureteral stones pass spontaneously during pregnancy. If pain is uncontrolled or infection develops, ureteral stenting provides drainage without stone treatment (preserves kidney function, relieves obstruction, defers definitive stone treatment until post-delivery). SWL is absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy. Non-contrast CT should be avoided if possible (radiation); ultrasound was already presumably performed to identify the stone.

wrong.A: SWL is absolutely contraindicated in ALL stages of pregnancy — shock waves cause direct fetal tissue injury through physical forces. No fetal shielding can protect against intracorporeal shock wave propagation.
wrong.C: Ureteroscopy under general/spinal anaesthesia is preferred over SWL if surgical intervention is required in pregnancy, but it carries anaesthetic risks. The question stem describes a stable patient with controlled symptoms — observation is appropriate first-line before surgical intervention.
wrong.D: Non-contrast CT involves ionising radiation and should be avoided in the first trimester. The initial investigation in pregnancy should be renal ultrasound; MRI if further imaging is needed. A stable patient with a presumed ureteral stone does not require CT.

explanation.guideline: EAU Guidelines on Urolithiasis 2024 — Pregnancy and urinary stones: SWL absolutely contraindicated; first-line = analgesia + observation; ureteral stent for uncontrolled pain/infection/obstruction of solitary kidney; ureteroscopy if stenting fails or stone passage fails
explanation.trial: White WM et al. J Urol 2007: Urolithiasis in pregnancy — 70–80% of pregnant women with ureteral stones pass spontaneously with conservative management; ureteroscopy is safe in all trimesters when necessary; SWL universally contraindicated
explanation.pearl: Pregnancy + ureteral stone: SWL NEVER, CT AVOID (use US/MRI), analgesia = paracetamol, observation first. Stent if obstruction causes problems. Ureteroscopy (not SWL) if intervention required. Defer definitive treatment post-delivery when possible.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man has a 12 mm left lower-pole renal stone with Hounsfield units of 1250 HU on CT. He is asking whether SWL is appropriate. Which statement most accurately predicts his SWL outcome?

options.A: Lower-pole position is the only factor — 12 mm lower-pole stones have 65–80% SWL stone-free rates
options.B: Stone density >1000 HU AND lower-pole location both predict poor SWL outcome — ureteroscopy or PCNL preferred
options.C: SWL achieves >85% stone-free rates for any renal stone ≤15 mm
options.D: Hounsfield unit measurement does not influence SWL outcome selection

explanation.correct: This patient has two independent predictors of poor SWL outcome: (1) Lower-pole location — gravity-dependent anatomy impairs fragment drainage; lower-pole SWL stone-free rates for 10–20 mm stones are approximately 45–60%; (2) High stone density (1250 HU) — calcium oxalate monohydrate stones (most common high-HU stone) are highly resistant to shock wave fragmentation. EAU 2024 considers HU >1000 a marker of SWL-resistant stone. With both factors present, f-URS (flexible ureteroscopy) or PCNL is recommended. EAU specifically recommends f-URS or PCNL over SWL for lower-pole stones 10–20 mm.

wrong.A: This underestimates the impact of stone density. The 65–80% figure applies to upper/middle calyx stones of this size — lower-pole 10–20 mm stones with high HU achieve much lower SWL stone-free rates (45–60% at best, likely lower with HU 1250).
wrong.C: >85% SWL stone-free rate applies to upper/middle calyx stones <10 mm. For 12 mm lower-pole stones, particularly those with high density, SWL outcomes are substantially worse.
wrong.D: Hounsfield unit measurement on CT is a validated predictor of SWL outcome — HU >1000 predicts SWL-resistant stone composition (CaOx monohydrate, brushite). EAU 2024 includes stone density in treatment selection guidance.

explanation.guideline: EAU Guidelines on Urolithiasis 2024 — Predictors of poor SWL outcome: stone size >20 mm, lower-pole location, high stone density (HU >1000), long skin-to-stone distance >100 mm, infundibulopelvic angle <70°. Combination of factors = recommend URS or PCNL.
explanation.trial: Pareek G et al. J Endourol 2005: CT stone density and SWL outcomes — stones >900 HU had significantly lower fragmentation rates (58% vs 92% for stones <900 HU); HU measurement should guide treatment selection
explanation.pearl: Two red flags for SWL failure: lower-pole location AND high HU (>1000). Either alone reduces SWL success — both together = strongly recommend URS or PCNL. Always measure CT HU before recommending SWL. HU >1000 = hard stone (CaOx monohydrate, brushite) = SWL-resistant.

questions.stem: A patient undergoes SWL for a 15 mm right renal pelvic stone. One week later, he presents with fever (39.2°C), right flank pain, and a plain KUB showing a column of stone fragments in the right ureter. What is this complication and how should it be managed?

options.A: Post-SWL urinary tract infection — oral antibiotics and discharge
options.B: Steinstrasse — evaluate for obstruction and infection; if septic/obstructed, urgent ureteroscopy or stenting
options.C: SWL-induced renal haematoma — CT abdomen and conservative management
options.D: Spontaneous ureter perforation — emergency surgery

explanation.correct: Steinstrasse ('stone street') is the accumulation of stone fragments in the ureter following SWL for renal stones, occurring in 2–4% (higher risk with larger stones). The column of fragments in the right ureter on KUB combined with fever and flank pain indicates obstructed steinstrasse with infection — a urological emergency requiring urgent drainage. Management: IV antibiotics + urgent ureteroscopy (laser fragmentation of the lead fragment to open the column) or retrograde ureteral stenting to decompress the obstruction. Asymptomatic steinstrasse without obstruction or infection can be observed (50–60% resolve spontaneously) or treated with MET (tamsulosin).

wrong.A: Oral antibiotics alone are insufficient when the ureter is obstructed by steinstrasse with concurrent infection — infected obstructed kidney requires urgent drainage, not antibiotics alone. Sending home is dangerous.
wrong.C: The KUB shows ureteral fragments — this is steinstrasse, not haematoma. Haematoma would not appear as a column of stones on KUB and would present with subcapsular pain without obstructive features.
wrong.D: The fragments in the ureter are SWL-fragmented stone fragments, not a spontaneous perforation. The ureter itself is intact — the stones are within the ureter lumen. Surgical perforation repair is not indicated.

explanation.guideline: EAU Guidelines on Urolithiasis 2024 — Steinstrasse: asymptomatic = observation + MET; symptomatic/obstructed without fever = URS or stenting; obstructed + fever (septic steinstrasse) = emergency drainage (URS or retrograde stent) + IV antibiotics
explanation.trial: Preminger GM et al. J Urol 1989: Steinstrasse management — 50% resolve spontaneously without intervention; lead fragment obstruction requires URS; septic steinstrasse requires immediate drainage; larger pre-treatment stones = higher steinstrasse risk
explanation.pearl: Steinstrasse = column of fragments obstructing the ureter after SWL. Septic steinstrasse = emergency drainage (stent or URS). Asymptomatic = observe or tamsulosin. Prevention: pre-SWL stent for large stones (>15 mm) or high-risk anatomy. Never discharge a febrile post-SWL patient without imaging and drainage if obstructed.

questions.stem: During flexible ureteroscopy for a 12 mm right renal pelvic stone, the surgeon is using a pneumatic lithotripter and notes the stone has disappeared from view after the first few bursts. Subsequent inspection shows the stone has migrated into the upper calyx. What was the mechanism and how should this complication be managed?

options.A: Thermal injury from the lithotripter caused stone vaporisation — no further action needed
options.B: Stone retropulsion from pneumatic lithotripsy — attempt flexible ureteroscope access to the upper calyx; if inaccessible, defer or convert to PCNL
options.C: Stone fragmentation was complete — fragments are too small to see
options.D: Ureteral avulsion has occurred — immediate open surgery required

explanation.correct: Pneumatic (ballistic) lithotripsy generates significant stone retropulsion — the mechanical impulse propels the stone away from the probe with each pulse, not just toward fragmentation. Stone migration into a calyx is a classic complication of pneumatic lithotripsy in the renal pelvis. Management: if using a flexible ureteroscope, attempt access to the upper calyx for further fragmentation/extraction. If the calyx is inaccessible or the stone is deeply impacted, options are: deferred flexible URS, or PCNL if access is not achievable endoscopically. Prevention: use Ho:YAG laser (lower retropulsion than pneumatic) or Moses pulse technology for intrarenal stones where retropulsion is a concern.

wrong.A: Pneumatic lithotripsy does not vaporise stones — it fragments them mechanically. A 12 mm stone cannot be vaporised by pneumatic energy in 2–3 pulses. The stone has migrated, not disappeared through vaporisation.
wrong.C: Complete fragmentation of a 12 mm stone in a few pulses with pneumatic lithotripsy is unlikely — typical fragmentation requires many pulses. Even if fragmented, fragments of a 12 mm stone would still be visible and would not fit into a calyx without migration.
wrong.D: Ureteral avulsion causes loss of ureteral lumen continuity and recognition of white tissue (ureteral wall). Stone migration into a calyx does not indicate ureteral avulsion — the ureteroscope remains in the lumen.

explanation.guideline: EAU Guidelines on Urolithiasis 2024 — Stone retropulsion: common with pneumatic lithotripsy; holmium laser (especially Moses pulse or TFL) has significantly lower retropulsion; intraoperative migration to calyx = attempt flexible URS or defer; prevention = laser preferred for intrarenal stones
explanation.trial: Bhagat SK et al. J Urol 2007: Retropulsion rates — pneumatic 21% vs Ho:YAG 5% for intraureteral/intrarenal stones; Moses pulse technology reduces Ho:YAG retropulsion further; established laser superiority for minimising stone migration
explanation.pearl: Pneumatic lithotripsy: effective but HIGH retropulsion. Use Ho:YAG laser for intrarenal or proximal ureteral stones where retropulsion would migrate the stone into an inaccessible calyx. Moses/TFL laser = lowest retropulsion. Pneumatic = acceptable for distal ureteral stones where retropulsion pushes stone proximally (manageable).

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with a 22 mm right renal pelvic stone is being discussed for surgical management. He has a normal left kidney, haemoglobin 14.2 g/dL, and eGFR 68 mL/min/1.73m². Stone composition from a prior passed fragment was calcium oxalate monohydrate. What is the EAU 2024 recommended primary treatment?

options.A: Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (SWL)
options.B: Flexible ureteroscopy with holmium laser (f-URS/RIRS)
options.C: Percutaneous nephrolithotomy (PCNL)
options.D: Open pyelolithotomy

explanation.correct: For renal stones >20 mm, PCNL is the EAU 2024 Grade A recommended primary treatment. Stone-free rates: PCNL 80–90% (single session) versus f-URS 60–75% (single session, often requiring multiple procedures for this stone size). SWL is inadequate for 22 mm stones (stone-free rate <50%) and calcium oxalate monohydrate (HU typically >1000, SWL-resistant). PCNL provides the highest single-session stone-free rate with one general anaesthetic and one procedure. Open pyelolithotomy is reserved for exceptional cases (failed PCNL, complex anatomy, concomitant anatomical reconstruction required).

wrong.A: SWL stone-free rate for a 22 mm CaOx monohydrate stone is <30–40% — inadequate. EAU 2024 does not recommend SWL as primary treatment for stones >20 mm. Multiple SWL sessions would be required with high risk of steinstrasse.
wrong.B: f-URS/RIRS is an alternative for patients who are unfit for PCNL (e.g., coagulopathy, anticoagulation, extreme obesity), but as a primary procedure it achieves lower single-session stone-free rates (60–75%) than PCNL (80–90%) for stones >20 mm. EAU Grade A recommendation is PCNL for this stone size.
wrong.D: Open pyelolithotomy is now a historical procedure reserved for exceptional circumstances (complex reconstruction, failed all endoscopic options, horseshoe kidney with extreme anatomy). Its role in primary stone management has been almost entirely replaced by PCNL.

explanation.guideline: EAU Guidelines on Urolithiasis 2024 — Renal stones >20 mm: PCNL Grade A primary recommendation. f-URS acceptable alternative for PCNL-unsuitable patients. SWL not recommended >20 mm. Open surgery: exceptional cases only.
explanation.trial: Srisubat A et al. Cochrane Database Syst Rev 2014: PCNL vs SWL vs URS for renal stones — PCNL significantly superior stone-free rates for stones >20 mm; PCNL 80–90% vs SWL 40–50% for stones this size; PCNL remains gold standard for large renal calculi
explanation.pearl: Stone >20 mm = PCNL (Grade A). Stone 10–20 mm = SWL or f-URS (equivalent for upper/middle; f-URS preferred for lower pole). Stone <10 mm = SWL or f-URS or observe. This size-based hierarchy is the core of EAU 2024 stone treatment recommendations.

questions.stem: What is the primary mechanism by which the holmium:YAG laser fragments urinary stones?

options.A: Photomechanical — laser energy causes direct stone material breakdown by photon absorption in the stone crystal lattice
options.B: Photothermal — rapid water absorption at 2140 nm wavelength creates a steam bubble (Moses effect) at the stone surface, causing localised explosive fracture
options.C: Cavitation — focused ultrasound waves create microbubbles that collapse and fragment the stone
options.D: Electrohydraulic — electrical spark creates plasma channel in water, generating compressive shock waves

explanation.correct: The holmium:YAG laser at 2140 nm wavelength is absorbed predominantly by water, not by stone material itself. The laser energy is absorbed in the thin water layer at the stone surface, rapidly vaporising that water to create a steam bubble (the 'Moses effect' or 'vapor bubble'). As this bubble expands and collapses, it creates localised explosive forces that fracture the stone surface — a photothermal mechanism. This is stone composition-independent because the energy is absorbed by the ubiquitous water film, not by the stone crystal structure. This distinguishes Ho:YAG from older colour-dependent lasers (pulsed dye 504 nm — only fragments yellow calcium stones).

wrong.A: Photomechanical fragmentation describes direct photon interaction with stone crystal lattice — this is not the primary Ho:YAG mechanism. Ho:YAG does not target the stone material directly but the water film at the interface.
wrong.C: Cavitation describes the mechanism of ultrasound-based lithotripsy (SWL) and ultrasonic endoscopic lithotripsy. Ho:YAG laser fragmentation is not primarily cavitation-based, though small cavitation bubbles form secondary to the primary photothermal event.
wrong.D: Electrohydraulic is a separate lithotripsy modality — electrical spark in saline creating a plasma channel and compressive hydraulic shock wave. This is not the Ho:YAG mechanism.

explanation.guideline: EAU Guidelines on Urolithiasis 2024 — Ho:YAG laser lithotripsy: photothermal mechanism at 2140 nm (water absorption coefficient highest in biological tissues); effective against all stone compositions; Moses pulse technology optimises bubble dynamics
explanation.trial: Chan KF et al. J Endourol 1999: Ho:YAG laser stone fragmentation mechanism — photothermal water absorption, vapor bubble formation, and stone surface microcrater explosion confirmed by high-speed photography; composition-independent fragmentation demonstrated
explanation.pearl: Ho:YAG (2140 nm) = absorbed by WATER not stone = photothermal = works on ALL stone compositions. The 'Moses effect' = laser opens a channel through water to bring energy directly to the stone surface. Contrast with pulsed dye laser (504 nm) = absorbed by stone pigment = only works on yellow calcium stones.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman undergoes flexible ureteroscopy for a 14 mm left mid-ureteral stone. Post-operatively at 6 hours, she is febrile (38.5°C), tachycardic (HR 118), hypotensive (BP 88/55 mmHg), and her blood cultures return Escherichia coli 24 hours later. Pre-operative urine culture had been positive for E. coli but the procedure was performed without pre-operative antibiotic treatment. What was the principal error and management now?

options.A: Ureteral perforation during the procedure — emergency surgical repair
options.B: Post-URS urosepsis from proceeding with positive pre-operative urine culture without treatment — resuscitate with IV fluids, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, ICU admission if required
options.C: Normal post-operative fever from surgical stress — acetaminophen and observation
options.D: Perinephric haematoma — CT scan to confirm and conservative management

explanation.correct: Post-ureteroscopy urosepsis from proceeding with an untreated positive pre-operative urine culture. EAU 2024 mandates pre-operative urine culture and antibiotic treatment of confirmed UTI before ureteroscopy — proceeding with active infection and endoscopy significantly increases intrarenal pressure (irrigation during URS), causing bacteraemia from pyelovenous/pyelolymphatic backflow. Management: immediate IV resuscitation (fluids, vasopressors if required), broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (covering gram-negatives including E. coli — piperacillin-tazobactam, meropenem, or as per local antimicrobial guidance), blood cultures before antibiotics, ICU/HDU level monitoring, and sepsis 6-bundle (NICE Sepsis Guideline).

wrong.A: Ureteral perforation presents intraoperatively with loss of vision, haematuria, and recognition of white tissue — not as delayed post-operative haemodynamic instability with fever and bacteraemia. This presentation is classic post-URS urosepsis.
wrong.C: Normal post-operative fever does not cause haemodynamic instability (BP 88/55, HR 118) and bacteraemia. A 6-hour post-operative fever with haemodynamic compromise in a patient with untreated pre-operative bacteriuria = urosepsis requiring emergency management.
wrong.D: Perinephric haematoma presents with flank pain and haematuria, not with fever and haemodynamic shock. Blood cultures would be negative in haematoma.

explanation.guideline: EAU Guidelines on Urolithiasis 2024 — Pre-operative urine culture MANDATORY before URS; treat confirmed UTI before proceeding; prophylactic antibiotics (single-dose gentamicin or trimethoprim) for all URS regardless of culture result; proceeding with positive culture = major adverse event
explanation.trial: De la Rosette J et al. J Urol 2014 (CROES URS Global Study): Post-URS urosepsis in 0.3% overall; rate increases to 5.5% in patients with positive pre-operative urine cultures not treated pre-operatively; established mandatory pre-operative urine culture policy
explanation.pearl: NEVER perform URS with a positive pre-operative urine culture without treating first. Post-URS sepsis: IV resuscitation + broad-spectrum antibiotics + ICU if required. The error: elevated intrarenal pressure during irrigation + infected urine = bacteraemia. Always culture urine before URS; treat before proceeding.

questions.stem: Following ureteroscopy with holmium laser for a 9 mm proximal ureteral stone, the surgeon places a ureteral stent. The patient is discharged and returns to clinic 3 weeks later complaining of significant urinary frequency (15 voids/day), urgency, haematuria on voiding, and dysuria. What is the most likely diagnosis and initial management?

options.A: Urinary tract infection — urine culture and antibiotics
options.B: Ureteral stent symptoms — alpha-blocker (tamsulosin) and planned stent removal
options.C: Ureteral stricture from laser injury — cystoscopy and urethrogram
options.D: Residual stone fragments causing obstruction — NCCT KUB

explanation.correct: Ureteral stent symptoms (frequency, urgency, haematuria, dysuria, flank pain on voiding, pelvic discomfort) occur in 60–80% of stented patients. These symptoms are from the ureteral stent coil in the bladder triggering trigonal irritation and OAB-like symptoms. Management: (1) Alpha-blocker (tamsulosin 0.4 mg OD) — reduces stent-related LUTS including urgency and frequency (EAU Grade B); (2) Antimuscarinics or mirabegron may be added for persistent OAB symptoms; (3) Plan stent removal at the agreed time (typically 2–4 weeks post-URS). Stent removal resolves symptoms in essentially all patients.

wrong.A: UTI is possible but stent symptoms are the much more common cause of frequency/urgency/dysuria after ureteral stent placement. Urine culture should be obtained to exclude UTI, but stent symptoms should be the primary diagnosis given the classic presentation timing (symptoms ongoing from stent placement) and the comprehensive symptom cluster.
wrong.C: Ureteral stricture from laser injury is a delayed complication presenting at 3–12 months post-URS, not at 3 weeks. At 3 weeks, the patient still has the stent in situ — the symptoms are stent-related, not from stricture.
wrong.D: Residual stone fragments causing obstruction would typically present with flank pain and hydronephrosis, not primarily with irritative LUTS. The symptom pattern (frequency, urgency, dysuria) is typical of stent irritation of the bladder trigone.

explanation.guideline: EAU Guidelines on Urolithiasis 2024 — Ureteral stent symptoms: 60–80% of patients experience significant LUTS; alpha-blocker (tamsulosin) Grade B recommendation for symptom relief; antimuscarinic as adjunct; planned stent removal at earliest safe time
explanation.trial: Lamb AD et al. J Urol 2011 (USSG trial): Tamsulosin significantly reduces ureteral stent symptoms vs placebo — IPSS storage subscore reduced by 35%; QoL significantly improved; established alpha-blocker as standard management for stent-related LUTS
explanation.pearl: Post-URS stent symptoms = extremely common (60–80%). Prescription at discharge: tamsulosin 0.4 mg OD for stent duration + planned stent removal date. Symptoms resolve with stent removal. Failing to prescribe tamsulosin or advise patients about stent symptoms is a common but avoidable quality-of-care failure.

questions.stem: During ureteroscopy for a 7 mm distal ureteral stone, when attempting to advance the semirigid ureteroscope past the vesicoureteric junction, significant resistance is encountered. After two attempts, the ureteroscope cannot be advanced beyond 2 cm into the ureter. What is the most appropriate management?

options.A: Force the ureteroscope past the resistance — the VUJ will dilate with pressure
options.B: Insert a ureteral balloon dilator and forcibly dilate the ureter
options.C: Place a ureteral access stent (passive dilation) for 2–4 weeks and reschedule ureteroscopy
options.D: Convert to open ureterolithotomy

explanation.correct: Failure to access the ureter (unable to advance the ureteroscope past the VUJ) occurs in 3–5% of ureteroscopy cases, most commonly in patients without prior ureteral stenting or instrumentation. The appropriate management is placement of a ureteral stent (access stent, 6 Fr) over the safety guidewire and scheduling a deferred ureteroscopy after 2–4 weeks of passive ureteral dilation. Passive dilation by the stent allows the ureter to expand enough for safe ureteroscope passage at the second attempt. Balloon dilation is an option but carries a higher risk of ureteral mucosal injury and potential stricture formation compared to passive dilation.

wrong.A: Forcing the ureteroscope against resistance risks ureteral avulsion — the most serious ureteroscopic complication. A rigid ureteroscope forced past a narrow VUJ can tear the ureter. This is absolutely contraindicated.
wrong.B: Balloon dilation is more traumatic than passive stent dilation and is associated with higher rates of mucosal stripping and delayed ureteral stricture. Passive stent dilation is the preferred EAU approach for failed access.
wrong.D: Open ureterolithotomy is rarely indicated in modern urology — a 7 mm distal stone in a patient who cannot be accessed ureteroscopically should receive a deferred ureteroscopy after passive dilation, not open surgery.

explanation.guideline: EAU Guidelines on Urolithiasis 2024 — Failed URS access: place ureteral access stent (passive dilation) and reschedule ureteroscopy at 2–4 weeks; balloon dilation as alternative but passive dilation preferred for reduced mucosal injury risk
explanation.trial: Denstedt JD & Razvi H. J Urol 1993: Passive ureteral dilation with pre-placed stent — 95% successful access at second ureteroscopy after 2-week stent dilation; balloon dilation associated with higher early stricture rates in comparative series
explanation.pearl: Failed URS access: PLACE STENT (passive dilation) for 2–4 weeks, reschedule. NEVER force ureteroscope past tight VUJ — ureteral avulsion risk. The safety guidewire is your friend — keep it in throughout and use it to place the stent if access fails.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man is counselled about flexible ureteroscopy (f-URS) versus SWL for a 9 mm upper-calyx right renal stone with HU 650. He is otherwise healthy, takes no anticoagulants, and has a normal contralateral kidney. Which statement correctly compares these two treatment options for this specific stone?

options.A: SWL is absolutely contraindicated for upper calyx stones — f-URS is mandatory
options.B: Both SWL and f-URS are acceptable first-line options with comparable stone-free rates (86–95%); shared decision-making is appropriate
options.C: f-URS always achieves higher stone-free rates than SWL for any renal stone
options.D: SWL should not be used for stones >5 mm — URS is the standard of care

explanation.correct: For a 9 mm upper-calyx renal stone with intermediate HU (650 — moderate density, reasonable SWL candidate), both SWL (stone-free rate 86–90% for upper-calyx stones <10 mm) and f-URS (stone-free rate 90–95%) are acceptable per EAU 2024. The choice should be made through shared decision-making considering patient preference (non-invasive vs. one procedure), institutional expertise, stone composition prediction, and logistical factors. EAU 2024 does not mandate one approach over the other for this clinical scenario. Patient preference for non-invasive treatment (SWL) or for single-procedure certainty (f-URS) are both valid drivers.

wrong.A: SWL is not contraindicated for upper-calyx stones — it achieves 86–90% stone-free rates in this location for stones <10 mm. There is no anatomical contraindication to SWL for upper calyx stones (unlike lower pole where drainage is a concern).
wrong.C: f-URS does NOT always achieve higher stone-free rates than SWL for any renal stone. For stones <10 mm in upper/middle calyces, both achieve comparable stone-free rates (86–95%). f-URS is clearly superior for lower pole stones and for stones with SWL-predictive characteristics (high HU, cystine composition).
wrong.D: SWL is recommended by EAU 2024 for stones up to 20 mm (upper/middle calyces). The statement that SWL should not be used for stones >5 mm is incorrect.

explanation.guideline: EAU Guidelines on Urolithiasis 2024 — Renal stones <10 mm in upper/middle calyces: SWL and f-URS are equivalent first-line options; shared decision-making essential. Guidance does not mandate one approach over the other in this size/location category.
explanation.trial: Geraghty RM et al. J Endourol 2018: Systematic review and meta-analysis URS vs SWL for ureteral and renal stones — for renal stones <10 mm: no statistically significant difference in stone-free rates; URS higher single-session stone-free rate; SWL preferred by patients seeking non-invasive approach
explanation.pearl: Upper calyx stone <10 mm: SWL (86–90%) = f-URS (90–95%) = equivalent. Choose based on patient preference, stone HU (high HU favours URS), and institutional expertise. Shared decision-making is the correct answer — there is no 'mandatory' single approach for this scenario.

questions.stem: A patient is found to have a 5 mm asymptomatic right upper-pole renal stone on ultrasound performed for an unrelated indication. He has never had a stone episode. Serum creatinine is normal, no urinary infection, contralateral kidney normal. What is the most appropriate initial management according to EAU 2024?

options.A: Immediate flexible ureteroscopy — all renal stones should be treated to prevent future colic
options.B: SWL — even asymptomatic stones should receive preventive SWL
options.C: Active surveillance with imaging at 6–12 months; intervention only if stone grows, becomes symptomatic, or passes to the ureter
options.D: Referral for dietary modification only — no imaging surveillance required

explanation.correct: For a small (<10 mm), asymptomatic, incidentally found renal stone in a patient without prior stone history and no high-risk features, EAU 2024 supports active surveillance with imaging surveillance at 6–12 months. Most small asymptomatic renal stones remain stable over years without intervention. Indications for intervention in asymptomatic stones: growth on surveillance imaging, progression to obstructing position, patient preference, high-risk occupation (pilot, diver), infection risk (immunosuppression), or stone composition predicting high growth rate (cystine, infection stone). Routine immediate treatment of all asymptomatic small stones is not evidence-based.

wrong.A: Immediate ureteroscopy for an asymptomatic 5 mm stone introduces procedural risks (anaesthesia, ureteral injury, infection) without clear benefit. EAU 2024 does not recommend routine immediate treatment of asymptomatic small renal stones.
wrong.B: SWL for an asymptomatic 5 mm stone similarly subjects the patient to unnecessary treatment. Observation is appropriate for small asymptomatic stones without high-risk features.
wrong.D: Dietary modification is appropriate as primary prevention, but no imaging surveillance is not acceptable — stone growth must be monitored. Annual imaging (ultrasound preferred over CT for surveillance to minimise radiation) is the EAU recommendation.

explanation.guideline: EAU Guidelines on Urolithiasis 2024 — Asymptomatic renal stones <10 mm: active surveillance with imaging at 6–12 months is appropriate. Intervention indicated for growth, symptoms, infection risk, or patient preference/occupation. Annual imaging surveillance with ultrasound preferred over CT for radiation minimisation.
explanation.trial: Orikasa S et al. Int J Urol 2006: Natural history of small asymptomatic renal stones — 51% stable at 3 years; 23% passed spontaneously; 26% grew requiring intervention; supports surveillance with selective intervention rather than routine early treatment
explanation.pearl: Asymptomatic stone <10 mm = active surveillance (not immediate treatment). EAU 2024: imaging at 6–12 months, then annually. Intervention if: growth, symptoms, infection risk, high-risk occupation. Ultrasound preferred for surveillance (no radiation) — CT only if surveillance ultrasound shows change.

questions.stem: The thulium fibre laser (TFL) is increasingly used for ureteroscopy. Compared to the standard holmium:YAG laser, what is the primary clinical advantage of TFL for stone fragmentation?

options.A: TFL fragments only uric acid stones — it is composition-specific
options.B: TFL uses 532 nm wavelength, identical to the neodymium:YAG laser
options.C: TFL (1940 nm) has superior water absorption, lower retropulsion, and higher stone fragmentation efficiency compared to standard Ho:YAG
options.D: TFL requires a rigid probe and cannot be used through flexible ureteroscopes

explanation.correct: The thulium fibre laser (TFL) operates at 1940 nm — the wavelength of maximum water absorption in biological tissues (compared to Ho:YAG at 2140 nm, which is slightly less efficient). This superior water absorption: (1) increases energy efficiency per pulse; (2) reduces thermal spread; (3) significantly reduces stone retropulsion compared to standard Ho:YAG (lower peak pressure forces). Early RCTs demonstrate TFL achieves comparable or superior stone-free rates to Ho:YAG in flexible ureteroscopy with less stone migration. TFL fibres are small enough for flexible ureteroscopes (as small as 150 μm).

wrong.A: TFL fragments all stone types, not just uric acid — it is composition-independent (same principle as Ho:YAG — water absorption, not stone-specific). Composition-specific lithotripsy is a property of historical colour-dependent lasers (504 nm pulsed dye), not modern near-infrared lasers.
wrong.B: TFL wavelength is 1940 nm — not 532 nm. The 532 nm (green light) wavelength describes GreenLight laser (KTP laser) used for photoselective vaporisation of the prostate (BPH treatment), not for stone fragmentation.
wrong.D: TFL uses flexible fibres (as small as 150 μm) compatible with flexible ureteroscopes. Its main advantage is IN flexible ureteroscopes where retropulsion causes stone migration — not a rigid probe instrument.

explanation.guideline: EAU Guidelines on Urolithiasis 2024 — Thulium fibre laser: emerging technology for URS; advantages over standard Ho:YAG include lower retropulsion and higher fragmentation efficiency; recommended for further study; comparable stone-free rates with reduced stone migration in early trials
explanation.trial: Corrales M et al. J Urol 2021: TFL vs Ho:YAG for flexible URS — TFL achieved higher stone clearance rate (83% vs 73% stone-free at 3 months) with significantly lower retropulsion events; TFL emerging as preferred laser for flexible URS in high-volume centres
explanation.pearl: TFL (1940 nm) vs Ho:YAG (2140 nm): TFL = superior water absorption = higher efficiency + lower retropulsion = less stone migration during flexible URS. TFL is compatible with flexible ureteroscopes (small fibre diameter). Emerging evidence shows superiority — expect increasing use.

questions.stem: A ureteral access sheath (UAS) is placed before flexible ureteroscopy for a 16 mm renal stone. What is the primary urological safety benefit of using a UAS during flexible ureteroscopy?

options.A: The UAS prevents stone fragments from passing into the bladder
options.B: The UAS reduces intrarenal pelvic pressure during irrigation, reducing the risk of pyelovenous backflow and post-operative sepsis
options.C: The UAS prevents the need for a post-operative ureteral stent
options.D: The UAS increases the maximum irrigant pressure, improving stone visualisation

explanation.correct: The primary safety benefit of the ureteral access sheath (UAS) during flexible ureteroscopy is reduction of intrarenal pelvic pressure during irrigation. The UAS provides a continuous outflow channel alongside the ureteroscope — as irrigant flows in through the ureteroscope working channel, it can simultaneously flow out through the UAS lumen. Without a UAS, outflow is limited to the narrow space around the ureteroscope within the ureter, causing pressure build-up in the renal pelvis. High intrarenal pressure (>30–40 cmH₂O) in a patient with bacteriuria or infected stones causes pyelovenous/pyelolymphatic backflow — the primary mechanism of post-URS urosepsis. UAS use significantly reduces intrarenal pressure, reducing sepsis risk.

wrong.A: The UAS does not prevent stone fragments from entering the bladder — fragments smaller than the UAS lumen (11–13 Fr) flow freely through it. The UAS facilitates fragment retrieval by allowing repeated ureteroscope passes, but does not trap fragments.
wrong.C: UAS use does not eliminate the need for a post-operative ureteral stent — the indication for stenting depends on procedural factors (ureteral injury, incomplete stone clearance, infection) rather than UAS use. Routine stenting after uncomplicated URS is not mandatory regardless of UAS use.
wrong.D: The UAS reduces (not increases) intrarenal pressure by providing an outflow channel. Increasing irrigant pressure without an outflow channel would be dangerous — this is the opposite of UAS function.

explanation.guideline: EAU Guidelines on Urolithiasis 2024 — UAS during flexible URS: reduces intrarenal pressure (primary safety mechanism); facilitates multiple ureteroscope passes; associated with lower post-operative fever rates; recommended for large stone burden, infectious context, or renal anatomy with impaired drainage
explanation.trial: Lildal SK et al. World J Urol 2017: UAS vs no UAS during flexible URS — UAS significantly reduces intrarenal pelvic pressure (mean 18 vs 41 cmH₂O); associated with lower systemic inflammatory response rates (2.6% vs 11.5% SIRS criteria); established UAS as pressure-reduction safety device
explanation.pearl: UAS = safety device for flexible URS. Primary benefit: reduces intrarenal pelvic pressure → reduces pyelovenous backflow → reduces post-URS sepsis risk. Essential for: infectious/struvite stones, long procedures, high stone burden, single kidneys. UAS also facilitates multiple ureteroscope passes for stone fragment retrieval.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man on warfarin for atrial fibrillation (INR 2.8) presents with a 6 mm proximal ureteral stone and moderate hydronephrosis. He has mild pain controlled with oral analgesia, no fever, creatinine 92 μmol/L. What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.A: Proceed with SWL — warfarin is not a contraindication
options.B: Emergency ureteroscopy despite anticoagulation — obstruction takes priority
options.C: Ureteral stent placement to relieve obstruction + bridge anticoagulation management before definitive stone treatment
options.D: Tamsulosin and observation for 4 weeks, then interval NCCT to assess stone status

explanation.correct: This patient has a 6 mm proximal ureteral stone with moderate hydronephrosis in a stable, non-septic patient on therapeutic anticoagulation (INR 2.8). Ureteral stenting (retrograde, J-J stent) provides immediate drainage and symptom relief without requiring haemostasis — stent placement is performed under spinal or local anaesthesia with minimal bleeding risk, and can be performed with the patient anticoagulated. This buys time for anticoagulation optimisation before definitive stone treatment (ureteroscopy or SWL after INR is corrected). SWL is contraindicated with uncorrected anticoagulation (high haematoma risk). Emergency URS through anticoagulated tissue risks significant haemorrhage.

wrong.A: SWL is contraindicated with uncontrolled anticoagulation — shock waves cause renal parenchymal haemorrhage (perinephric haematoma) potentiated by anticoagulation. SWL should only be performed when INR is corrected to <1.5 (bridging with LMWH if necessary).
wrong.B: Proceeding with ureteroscopy at INR 2.8 carries high risk of significant haemorrhage and poor visualisation. Emergency URS is appropriate only for infected obstructed kidney or acute renal failure — this patient is stable. A stent provides adequate decompression while anticoagulation is managed.
wrong.D: Observation for 4 weeks with tamsulosin is appropriate for uncomplicated distal ureteral stones without anticoagulation concerns. A 6 mm proximal ureteral stone with moderate hydronephrosis on anticoagulation requires decompression rather than prolonged observation.

explanation.guideline: EAU Guidelines on Urolithiasis 2024 — Anticoagulated patients with ureteral stones: ureteral stenting is the preferred initial management to relieve obstruction while anticoagulation is managed; SWL contraindicated in uncorrected coagulopathy; definitive stone treatment after anticoagulation optimised
explanation.trial: Xu N et al. Urology 2019: Ureteral stenting for anticoagulated patients with obstructing ureteral stones — safe and effective temporary drainage strategy; definitive URS after anticoagulation management achieved 95% stone-free rate without increased haemorrhagic complications
explanation.pearl: Anticoagulated patient + obstructing stone = stent first, fix anticoagulation, then treat stone. SWL is contraindicated with uncorrected coagulopathy. Ureteral stenting can be performed safely under anticoagulation — minimal soft tissue dissection, no major vessel proximity. Plan definitive treatment after INR correction.

questions.stem: Following SWL for a 14 mm renal stone, imaging at 6 weeks shows multiple fragments with the largest being 3 mm. The patient is asymptomatic. According to EAU 2024, how should these fragments be managed?

options.A: Immediate ureteroscopy to clear all fragments — stone-free status must be achieved
options.B: Clinically insignificant residual fragments (CIRFs <4 mm) can be observed, but with active surveillance imaging at 6–12 months given 20–40% grow or cause symptoms at 5 years
options.C: Fragments <4 mm always pass spontaneously — no follow-up needed
options.D: Repeat SWL immediately to fragment residual stones further

explanation.correct: Clinically insignificant residual fragments (CIRFs) are defined as fragments <4 mm on post-treatment imaging. The largest fragment here is 3 mm — within CIRF definition. However, EAU 2024 notes that CIRFs are not truly insignificant: 20–40% grow, cause symptoms, or require intervention over 5-year follow-up. Active surveillance with imaging at 6–12 months is therefore recommended. Tamsulosin may facilitate passage of small fragments. If fragments grow beyond 4–5 mm or become symptomatic, intervention should be planned.

wrong.A: Immediate ureteroscopy for 3 mm fragments is excessive and introduces procedural risk for fragments that have a high chance of spontaneous passage. CIRFs <4 mm are managed with observation and surveillance, not immediate re-intervention.
wrong.C: While many CIRFs do pass spontaneously, 20–40% do not and may cause future problems. 'Always pass spontaneously — no follow-up' is incorrect and contradicts EAU 2024 evidence. Active surveillance is essential.
wrong.D: Repeat SWL for 3 mm fragments is not indicated — these fragments are small enough to pass without further fragmentation, and repeat SWL carries cumulative renal parenchymal injury risk. Repeat SWL is considered when the largest residual fragment is >5–7 mm and the stone appears to be SWL-responsive.

explanation.guideline: EAU Guidelines on Urolithiasis 2024 — CIRFs (<4 mm): not truly clinically insignificant; 20–40% grow or cause symptoms at 5 years; active surveillance with imaging at 6–12 months recommended; tamsulosin may facilitate passage; intervention if growth >4–5 mm or symptoms develop
explanation.trial: El-Nahas AR et al. J Urol 2008: Natural history of clinically insignificant residual fragments — 39% of CIRFs grew at 5-year follow-up; 25% required additional intervention; 'wait and see' without surveillance misses significant number of patients who develop complications
explanation.pearl: CIRFs (<4 mm): observe but DO NOT ignore. 20–40% grow or cause problems at 5 years. EAU 2024: surveillance imaging at 6–12 months for all CIRFs. CIRF is NOT the same as stone-free — document as 'residual fragments, under surveillance' in clinical notes.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man has a 4 mm stone in the lower calyx of his left kidney found incidentally. CT HU is 850. Infundibulopelvic angle on CT urogram is 55°. He is a commercial airline pilot. What treatment approach is most appropriate?

options.A: Annual surveillance — asymptomatic 4 mm stone requires no intervention
options.B: SWL — first-line for small lower-pole stones
options.C: Flexible ureteroscopy — first-line for lower-pole stones with poor drainage anatomy; additionally, his occupation requires reliable stone-free status
options.D: PCNL — required for all lower-pole stones regardless of size

explanation.correct: Two factors modify the standard 'observe a 4 mm asymptomatic stone' recommendation: (1) Lower-pole anatomy — infundibulopelvic angle of 55° (<70°) predicts very poor gravity-dependent drainage of stone fragments, making SWL outcome particularly poor (even for 4 mm stones, lower-pole stones with acute infundibulopelvic angle have 30–50% stone-free rates with SWL); (2) Occupation — commercial airline pilots require reliable stone-free status as per aviation medical authority regulations, as an acute colic episode during flight would be immediately hazardous. Flexible ureteroscopy with laser lithotripsy achieves 90–95% stone-free rates regardless of calyceal anatomy and provides definitive treatment. The combination of poor anatomical prediction for SWL success and occupational requirement for stone-free status makes f-URS the appropriate choice.

wrong.A: Annual surveillance without intervention is appropriate for asymptomatic stones generally, but not when occupation requires stone-free status (pilot, diver, military personnel). EAU 2024 includes high-risk occupation as an indication for active treatment even of small asymptomatic stones.
wrong.B: SWL for a lower-pole stone with infundibulopelvic angle 55° and HU 850 (moderately dense) is likely to result in inadequate stone clearance (acute angle impairs fragment drainage). For a pilot who needs confirmed stone-free status, the lower stone-free rate of SWL in this anatomical setting is unacceptable.
wrong.D: PCNL is not indicated for a 4 mm lower-pole stone. PCNL carries significant morbidity and is reserved for stones >20 mm. f-URS achieves comparable lower-pole stone-free rates to PCNL for small stones without the need for percutaneous access.

explanation.guideline: EAU Guidelines on Urolithiasis 2024 — High-risk occupations (pilots, military, remote workers): active stone treatment indicated even for small asymptomatic stones. Lower-pole stones with infundibulopelvic angle <70°: f-URS or PCNL preferred over SWL (poor drainage of SWL fragments).
explanation.trial: Albala DM et al. J Urol 2001: Lower-pole anatomy and SWL outcomes — infundibulopelvic angle <90° significantly reduces SWL stone-free rates; acute angle <70° is the strongest anatomical predictor of poor SWL clearance in lower pole
explanation.pearl: Lower-pole stone treatment decision: check infundibulopelvic angle (>90° = SWL acceptable; <70° = poor SWL drainage = prefer f-URS). For pilots/high-risk occupations: treat even asymptomatic stones. An acute infundibulopelvic angle + pilot occupation = f-URS is the clear choice.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man on aspirin 100 mg/day (cardioprotective) requires ureteroscopy for a 9 mm distal ureteral stone. Should aspirin be stopped before the procedure?

options.A: Yes — aspirin must always be stopped 7 days before any urological procedure to prevent bleeding
options.B: No — aspirin does not increase bleeding risk for endoscopic urological procedures and should be continued for cardiac protection
options.C: Continue aspirin if high cardiovascular risk (recent coronary stent, MI <12 months); consider stopping for 5–7 days if low cardiovascular risk; always assess with cardiologist/prescribing physician
options.D: Switch to therapeutic warfarin before ureteroscopy to provide better anticoagulation during the procedure

explanation.correct: EAU 2024 and perioperative anticoagulation guidelines: aspirin management before ureteroscopy should be individualised. Low-dose aspirin for secondary cardiovascular prevention carries real discontinuation risks — myocardial infarction and stent thrombosis (particularly within 12 months of coronary stent placement). For low cardiovascular risk (primary prevention, remote MI), aspirin can be stopped 5–7 days pre-procedure if bleeding risk is a concern. For high cardiovascular risk (recent coronary stent <12 months, recent MI, recurrent cardiac events), aspirin should be continued — ureteroscopy bleeding risk on aspirin alone is modest and typically manageable. Cardiologist input is mandatory before stopping aspirin in high-risk patients. Never switch to warfarin perioperatively for a procedure that aspirin alone was being used for.

wrong.A: Blanket cessation of all aspirin 7 days before any urological procedure is not current EAU guidance and risks serious cardiovascular events in high-risk patients. Risk-stratified aspirin management is required.
wrong.B: Aspirin does modestly increase bleeding risk at the procedural site (ureteroscope insertion, stone fragmentation site). While it rarely causes serious bleeding in URS, it is not completely risk-free. The statement that aspirin 'does not increase bleeding risk' is inaccurate.
wrong.D: Switching to therapeutic warfarin (anticoagulant) from aspirin (antiplatelet) before URS makes no clinical sense — this would dramatically increase haemorrhagic risk. Anticoagulants and antiplatelets are different drug classes serving different indications.

explanation.guideline: EAU Perioperative Anticoagulant Guidelines 2024 — Low-dose aspirin: continue for high cardiovascular risk; consider stopping 5–7 days for low cardiovascular risk before procedures with haemorrhagic risk; always involve prescribing physician/cardiologist. Risk-benefit assessment is mandatory.
explanation.trial: Oskarsson M et al. BJU Int 2015: Aspirin and ureteroscopy outcomes — aspirin continuation associated with minimal increase in haemorrhagic complications for URS; stopping aspirin in patients with cardiovascular risk not justified by modest URS bleeding risk; supports risk-stratified management
explanation.pearl: Aspirin before URS: individualised decision. High CV risk (recent stent, recent MI) = continue aspirin (stent thrombosis risk > URS bleeding risk). Low CV risk = consider stopping 5–7 days. Always discuss with cardiologist before stopping aspirin in anyone with coronary artery disease. Never switch to warfarin for an aspirin indication.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman undergoes ureteroscopy for a 7 mm left proximal ureteral stone. During the procedure, at maximum deflection of the flexible ureteroscope, the surgeon can identify the stone in the upper calyx. After laser fragmentation to 2 mm fragments, what is the EAU 2024 recommendation regarding routine ureteral stent placement at the end of this uncomplicated procedure?

options.A: Stent must always be placed after ureteroscopy — not placing a stent is a safety violation
options.B: Stent placement is optional after uncomplicated flexible ureteroscopy without ureteral injury — stentless URS is acceptable
options.C: Stent should only be avoided in paediatric patients — all adult URS requires stenting
options.D: Stent should be placed if the stone was >5 mm — size determines stent necessity

explanation.correct: EAU 2024 recommends that routine ureteral stenting after uncomplicated ureteroscopy is NOT mandatory. Stentless ureteroscopy is acceptable when: (1) no ureteral injury occurred; (2) no significant mucosal trauma; (3) stone clearance was adequate; (4) no pre-existing obstruction or infection; (5) normal contralateral kidney. Stent is indicated after URS when: ureteral injury (perforation, mucosal stripping), incomplete stone clearance with steinstrasse risk, significant mucosal oedema, pregnancy, solitary kidney, or concurrent infection. Omitting the stent avoids stent-related LUTS in 60–80% of patients and reduces healthcare burden.

wrong.A: Routine mandatory stent placement after all URS is not current EAU guidance. Multiple RCTs demonstrate that omitting the stent after uncomplicated URS is safe and improves patient-reported outcomes by avoiding stent symptoms.
wrong.C: Adult patients do not have a categorical requirement for stenting after URS. EAU guidelines apply equally to adult and paediatric populations — stent placement is based on procedural complications and individual factors, not patient age.
wrong.D: Stone size does not determine stent necessity per EAU 2024 — the procedural factors (ureteral injury, clearance completeness, mucosal condition) are the relevant determinants.

explanation.guideline: EAU Guidelines on Urolithiasis 2024 — Post-URS stent: not routinely required after uncomplicated URS (Grade B). Stent indicated: ureteral injury, incomplete clearance, infection, single kidney, significant mucosal trauma, pregnancy. RCTs demonstrate equivalent outcomes with and without stent in uncomplicated URS.
explanation.trial: Netto NR et al. J Urol 1997: Stent vs no stent after uncomplicated URS — equivalent stone-free rates and complication rates; stented patients had significantly more LUTS and reduced quality of life; established stentless URS as acceptable safe option
explanation.pearl: Post-URS stent: NOT always required. EAU Grade B: stentless URS acceptable after uncomplicated procedure. Stent IS indicated for: ureteral injury, incomplete clearance, infection, single kidney, pregnancy. Stents cause significant LUTS in 60–80% — avoid when safe. Always prescribe tamsulosin if stent is placed.

questions.stem: During ureteroscopy for a 10 mm proximal ureteral stone, the surgeon uses a nitinol basket to extract a 7 mm fragment. While extracting the basket, resistance is felt that increases progressively. The surgeon pulls harder but the basket cannot be withdrawn. What should be done next?

options.A: Continue pulling forcefully — the ureter will stretch to accommodate the fragment
options.B: Immediately release the basket and laser-fragment the captured stone until it is small enough to extract or dust it further — never pull with excessive force
options.C: Remove the ureteroscope together with the basket and fragment in one movement
options.D: Push the fragment back into the renal pelvis by pushing the basket further in

explanation.correct: Excessive traction on a basket containing a fragment too large for the ureter is the direct mechanism of ureteral avulsion — the most serious ureteroscopic complication. When resistance is encountered during basket extraction, the correct response is to STOP pulling, release or open the basket, and laser-fragment the captured fragment further until all pieces are small enough to pass the ureter safely. 'If in doubt, dust' — the urologist should fragment more aggressively until fragment size is guaranteed to be <3 mm before any extraction attempt. Never pull against resistance.

wrong.A: The ureter does NOT stretch sufficiently to accommodate oversized fragments without injury. Forceful traction causes ureteral avulsion — separation of the ureter from its wall, a catastrophic complication requiring immediate surgical repair.
wrong.C: Removing the ureteroscope together with the basket and fragment creates the same mechanical force as pulling the basket alone — this would cause the same ureteral avulsion. The ureteroscope provides additional force to the traction.
wrong.D: Pushing the fragment back is not the primary answer — the correct primary action is to release/open the basket and fragment further. Pushing back may be appropriate as a temporising measure if the stone is truly stuck, but the priority is to fragment the stone to safe-extraction size.

explanation.guideline: EAU Guidelines on Urolithiasis 2024 — Ureteral avulsion prevention: NEVER pull a basket against resistance; fragment stone further if extraction resistance encountered; urologists should be trained to recognise ureteral resistance and respond by additional fragmentation, not increased force
explanation.trial: Schuster TG et al. Urology 2001: Ureteral injuries during ureteroscopy — 7/8 ureteral avulsions caused by forceful basket extraction against resistance; all preventable with additional laser fragmentation before extraction; established 'fragment before extracting large pieces' as safety principle
explanation.pearl: Ureteral avulsion prevention: NEVER pull a basket with increasing resistance. Rule: 'If it doesn't come easily, fragment more.' Fragment the captured stone to <3 mm before extraction. Ureteral avulsion = catastrophic complication requiring immediate surgery. The safety guidewire: always keep in situ — it provides access for stent placement if avulsion occurs.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a previous left nephrectomy for renal cell carcinoma presents with a 13 mm lower-pole stone in his solitary right kidney. He is asymptomatic and creatinine is 98 μmol/L. Which treatment approach is most appropriate?

options.A: Annual surveillance — all asymptomatic stones in a solitary kidney should be observed
options.B: SWL monotherapy — non-invasive approach is safest for a solitary kidney
options.C: Flexible ureteroscopy or PCNL — active treatment recommended to prevent obstruction in a solitary kidney; lower-pole location and size favour f-URS or PCNL over SWL
options.D: Antibiotic prophylaxis and observation — solitary kidney patients are at higher infection risk

explanation.correct: A solitary kidney with a 13 mm lower-pole renal stone represents an indication for active treatment (not surveillance). Rationale: (1) Obstruction of a solitary kidney causes acute renal failure — no contralateral reserve exists; (2) Asymptomatic does not mean low-risk — colic and obstruction can occur unpredictably; (3) 13 mm lower-pole stone is above the usual observation threshold (>10 mm). Treatment choice: SWL achieves poor stone-free rates for lower-pole stones of this size (45–60%), with risk of steinstrasse causing acute solitary kidney failure. f-URS achieves 80–90% stone-free rate for 10–20 mm lower-pole stones. PCNL achieves 85–95% but with greater access risk. f-URS is the preferred approach balancing efficacy and risk in a solitary kidney patient.

wrong.A: Solitary kidney with a 13 mm lower-pole stone is NOT appropriate for surveillance. EAU 2024 recommends active treatment for stones in a solitary kidney — obstruction of the only functioning kidney causes acute renal failure with no renal reserve.
wrong.B: SWL is relatively contraindicated for this patient — not only does it have poor stone-free rates for lower-pole 13 mm stones (45–60%), but steinstrasse formation in a solitary kidney could cause complete obstruction and acute renal failure. The risk/benefit ratio favours a higher-certainty endoscopic procedure.
wrong.D: Antibiotic prophylaxis does not prevent stone growth or obstruction. Observation is not appropriate for a 13 mm stone in a solitary kidney.

explanation.guideline: EAU Guidelines on Urolithiasis 2024 — Solitary kidney: active treatment recommended for stones >10 mm or any obstructing stone; SWL lower-pole stones have poor outcomes + steinstrasse risk = avoid SWL for solitary kidney lower-pole stones >10 mm; f-URS or PCNL preferred
explanation.trial: Albala DM et al. J Urol 2001: Lower-pole stone outcomes in special populations — solitary kidney patients with lower-pole stones treated by SWL had significantly higher rates of acute obstruction from steinstrasse than those treated with URS; f-URS/PCNL recommended for lower-pole stones in solitary kidney
explanation.pearl: Solitary kidney + lower-pole stone >10 mm = active treatment mandatory (not surveillance), f-URS or PCNL preferred (not SWL — steinstrasse risk in solitary kidney = acute renal failure). Protect the only functioning kidney by choosing the treatment with the highest stone-free rate and lowest obstruction complication rate.
